title: Embedding
description: A dense vector that represents a token or other discrete item so the model can run continuous math on it.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: An embedding is a fixed-size list of numbers (a vector) associated with a discrete symbol such as a token ID. In transformer language models, the input embedding layer converts each position's token ID into a vector of model width (hidden size).

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Embeddings are where text becomes geometry: similar tokens can end up with nearby vectors after training, and every later layer operates on those vectors. Context length, batching, and memory all count embedding rows when you size a deployment.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: Suppose token ID 42 maps to a 768-dimensional vector. A sequence of three tokens becomes a 3×768 tensor of embeddings before the first attention block runs.

whereItAppears.title: Where It Appears
whereItAppears.body: Embeddings sit at the bottom of decoder-only transformers, in encoder inputs for seq2seq models, and in retrieval systems that compare query and document vectors. Positional information is usually added to embeddings before attention.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: An embedding is not the same as a one-hot vector: the table stores dense learned weights rather than a single 1 in a huge sparse vector. Embeddings are also not logits or probabilities—they are inputs, not outputs of the vocabulary head.

openingSummary: An embedding table maps each discrete token ID to a learned vector so the model can mix information in continuous space before deeper layers run.
